schools.summary: Chrysostom: when Paul has met what was pressing and upon his hands, and is about to enter on things perpetual which they ought continually to hear, he adds 'finally,' that is, always and forever; and, strange to say, he does not even speak of himself as of sufficient credit to exhort, but says 'we beseech and exhort you in the Lord' (though who was so worthy?); Paul beseeching and exhorting the brethren, by the Lord Jesus, that as they had received how to walk and please God, so they would abound more and more — the humble plea to advance ever further in a God-pleasing life (Paul, with the lowliness of one who entreats rather than commands, and that 'by the Lord Jesus,' urging the Thessalonians not to rest content with the holy walk they had learned but to grow in it, abounding 'more and more,' the Christian life a continual advance in pleasing God).

themes: we beseech you... and exhort you by the Lord Jesus
as ye have received of us how ye ought to walk and to please God
so ye would abound more and more
the Christian life a continual advance

schools.summary: Gill: the commandments Paul gave when among them — such as those of faith and love, the ordinances of the gospel (baptism and the Lord's supper), all that relate to the worship and service of God, to the discipline of Christ's house, to their behaviour one toward another, and their conduct in the world — were delivered not as from themselves but by the Lord Jesus; Paul reminding them that they know what commandments he gave them by the Lord Jesus — the apostolic precepts carrying Christ's own authority (Paul grounding his exhortation in the commandments they had already received, marking these were not his own private rules but precepts given 'by the Lord Jesus,' by his authority and in his name, so that to obey them was to obey Christ himself, and the holy walk he urged rested on Christ's own command).

themes: for ye know what commandments we gave you
by the Lord Jesus
the precepts carrying Christ's own authority
to obey them is to obey Christ

schools.summary: Gill: sanctification is internal or external — internal sanctification the work of the Spirit of God, a principle of spiritual life in the soul, a divine light in the understanding, a flexion of the will to the will of God, and a settlement of the affections on him; here especially holiness of life is meant, of which abstaining from fornication is a part; Paul declaring that this is the will of God, even their sanctification, that they abstain from fornication — the will of God for holiness, beginning with sexual purity (Paul enforcing his call to a God-pleasing walk by naming God's own will: that his people be sanctified, set apart and made holy, and singling out as a first and chief instance the abstaining from 'fornication,' all unlawful sexual intercourse, a sin to which the heathen world was wholly given but from which the holy God calls his people to keep clean).

themes: for this is the will of God, even your sanctification
that ye should abstain from fornication
God's will for holiness
beginning with sexual purity

schools.summary: Gill: by 'vessel' may be meant either a man's wife or his body, and it is not easy to determine which, for the Jews call both by this name (a woman being called a 'vessel,' and the body also); either way, the believer is to possess it (govern, keep, or use it) in sanctification and honour; Paul charging that each should know how to possess his vessel in sanctification and honour — the holy government of body or wife in purity (Paul, applying the call to purity, bidding each believer learn to 'possess his vessel,' whether his own body, keeping it pure and under control, or his wife, treating the marriage relation with chastity and respect, and that 'in sanctification,' holiness, and 'honour,' with reverence and decency, the opposite of the shameful indulgence of lust).

debates.question: What is the 'vessel' each is to possess in sanctification and honour?

sides.position: Either a man's own body (kept pure and under control) or his wife (the marriage relation treated with chastity and respect) — the Jews calling both by this name

themes: that every one of you should know how to possess his vessel
in sanctification and honour
the holy government of body or wife
against the indulgence of lust

schools.summary: Gill: to possess the vessel 'not in the lust of concupiscence' (or 'passion of lust') is not for the mere gratifying and indulging of that, not to cherish the sin of concupiscence, the first motions of sin in the heart by which a man is drawn away and enticed, not to blow up the flame of lust or make provision for the flesh to fulfil its lusts; Paul charging that the vessel be possessed not in the lust of concupiscence, as the Gentiles who know not God — the warning against heathen-like indulgence of lust (Paul setting the believer's holy use of the vessel against the very opposite, the unrestrained 'lust of concupiscence,' the passionate gratifying of evil desire, which marks 'the Gentiles which know not God,' for ignorance of God and slavery to lust go together, the heathen, lacking the knowledge and fear of God, giving themselves up to sensuality, from which the believer is wholly to differ).

themes: not in the lust of concupiscence
even as the Gentiles which know not God
the warning against heathen-like lust
ignorance of God and slavery to lust together

schools.summary: Gill: this is commonly understood of transgressing the bounds of justice and equity between men, of cheating and defrauding in trade and business (by increasing or lessening prices, not keeping to the bargain, contract, or covenant), though some connect it still with the matter of impurity (wronging a brother in his marriage); Paul charging that no man go beyond or defraud his brother in any matter, since the Lord is the avenger of all such, as he had forewarned them — the warning against injustice backed by God's avenging judgment (Paul, whether still on the theme of impurity or now of fraud in dealings, forbidding any going beyond the bounds of right to overreach and defraud another, and enforcing it with the solemn reminder that 'the Lord is the avenger' of all such wrongs, who will himself punish the oppressor, a warning Paul had given before).

themes: that no man go beyond and defraud his brother in any matter
because that the Lord is the avenger of all such
the warning against injustice
God himself avenging all such wrongs

schools.summary: Gill: this is another reason against unclean practices, taken from the end of the effectual calling by the efficacious grace of God, which is not to uncleanness; Barnes: when God called us to be his followers, it was not that we should be impure; Paul declaring that God has not called us unto uncleanness but unto holiness — the calling that aims at holiness not impurity (Paul pressing his charge home by the very nature and design of the Christian calling: God, in calling his people by his grace, did not call them to a life of 'uncleanness,' impurity and lust, but 'unto holiness,' sanctity of heart and life, so that to live in impurity is to contradict the whole purpose of the calling, which was to make them holy).

themes: for God hath not called us unto uncleanness
but unto holiness
the holy end of the calling
impurity contradicts the calling's purpose

schools.summary: Gill: he that rejects these things with contempt (the exhortations now delivered, the commandments given by the Lord Jesus Christ, and the will of God declared), takes no notice of them, and acts not according to them, despises not man but God, for the precepts are not human but divine; Paul declaring that he who despises these things despises not man but God, who has given us his Holy Spirit — the divine weight of the precepts and the Spirit who sanctifies (Paul clinching his charge to holiness by warning that whoever rejects and contemns these commands is not slighting a mere man, Paul or any teacher, but God himself, whose will and word they are, and adding the further solemnity that God 'hath given unto us his holy Spirit,' the very Spirit of holiness, whose presence both empowers the sanctity required and is grieved and affronted by the impurity forbidden).

themes: he therefore that despiseth, despiseth not man, but God
who hath also given unto us his holy Spirit
despising the precepts is despising God
the Spirit of holiness grieved by impurity

schools.summary: Chrysostom: why, having discoursed earnestly of chastity, and about to discourse of the duty of working and not sorrowing for the departed, does Paul introduce that which was the principal of all good things, love, as if passing it over, saying 'we have no need to write to you'? This also is from his great wisdom and belongs to spiritual instruction, for praising them for what they had makes them more zealous than rebuking them for what they lacked; Paul saying that, as to brotherly love, they need not be written to, for they are taught of God to love one another — the love that God himself implants needing no further command (Paul, turning from purity to love, paying the Thessalonians the high compliment of saying he need not write of brotherly love, since they 'are taught of God,' the Holy Spirit himself having inwardly taught them this grace, writing the law of love on their hearts, so their love was not a mere lesson learned from men but a thing divinely wrought in them).

themes: as touching brotherly love ye need not that I write
ye yourselves are taught of God to love one another
the brotherly love taught of God
the Spirit writing the law of love on their hearts

schools.summary: Gill: they exercised this grace of love without respect of persons — whether high or low, rich or poor, bond or free, greater or lesser believers, related in the bonds of nature or not — toward all, and not only all the brethren in the particular community at Thessalonica but in all Macedonia; Paul observing that they did love toward all the brethren in all Macedonia, but beseeching them to increase more and more — the broad love commended yet urged to grow (Paul, having praised their love as taught of God, adding proof of it, that they showed it not narrowly but toward all the brethren throughout the whole province, yet, true to his pattern, not letting even a real grace rest, but entreating them still to 'increase more and more,' for in love there is always room to grow).

themes: ye do it toward all the brethren which are in all Macedonia
that ye increase more and more
broad love commended yet urged to grow
in love there is always room to grow

schools.summary: Gill: to 'study to be quiet' is to live peaceably in their own families, to give no disturbance to other families by talebearing, whispering, and backbiting, to behave with quietness in the neighbourhood or city they dwell in, to seek its peace, and to lead a quiet and peaceable life in all godliness and honesty; Paul charging them to study to be quiet, to do their own business, and to work with their own hands, as he had commanded — the call to a peaceable, industrious, self-reliant life (Paul, perhaps correcting an idle restlessness occasioned by excitement over Christ's coming, bidding the believers make it their ambition, to 'study,' earnestly endeavour, to live quietly, minding their own affairs rather than meddling in others', and labouring honestly with their own hands to support themselves, the very opposite of an idle, busybody, or disorderly walk).

themes: that ye study to be quiet
and to do your own business
to work with your own hands, as we commanded you
a peaceable, industrious, self-reliant life

schools.summary: Gill: to 'walk honestly' is to walk decently, in good credit and reputation, providing things honest in the sight of all men for themselves and families, and honestly paying every man his own; on which account it became them to mind their own business and work at their trades, otherwise their walk would be scandalous; Paul giving the aim — that they walk honestly toward those without, and lack nothing — the twofold benefit of diligent living (Paul naming the ends served by the quiet, working life: first, 'that ye may walk honestly toward them that are without,' that their conduct might be honourable and reputable in the eyes of unbelievers, giving no occasion of reproach but a good witness, and second, 'that ye may have lack of nothing,' that, supporting themselves by their labour, they might be independent and free from want, not a burden on others).

themes: that ye may walk honestly toward them that are without
that ye may have lack of nothing
a good witness toward unbelievers
freedom from want, not a burden on others

schools.summary: Chrysostom: there are many things which from ignorance alone cause us sorrow, so that if we understand them well we banish our grief; this Paul shows, saying 'I would not have you ignorant, that ye sorrow not, even as the rest which have no hope'; Paul declaring that he would not have them ignorant concerning those who are asleep, that they sorrow not as others who have no hope — the cure of grief in the knowledge of the believing dead (Paul, turning to console the bereaved, naming death gently as a 'sleep,' for the believer's death is but a sleep from which he will surely wake, and dispelling their ignorance, the root of their grief; not that Christians may not mourn at all, but that they may not sorrow 'as others which have no hope,' the heathen, who, knowing nothing of resurrection, grieved without comfort, for the believer's hope wholly changes the character of his mourning).

themes: I would not have you to be ignorant... concerning them which are asleep
that ye sorrow not, even as others which have no hope
death but a sleep for the believer
hope changing the character of mourning

schools.summary: Gill: both the death and resurrection of Christ are fundamental articles of faith — nothing more certain, more comfortable, and more firmly to be believed than that Christ died for the sins of his people and rose again for their justification, on which depend the present peace, joy, and comfort of the saints; Paul reasoning that, if we believe Jesus died and rose again, even so those who sleep in Jesus will God bring with him — the risen Christ the pledge of his sleeping people's resurrection (Paul grounding the comfort in the gospel's heart: since it is the settled faith of all Christians that Jesus truly died and rose again, the same certainty assures us that 'them also which sleep in Jesus,' those who died in faith and union with him, 'will God bring with him,' God raising them and bringing them in Christ's train at his coming, so the resurrection of the Head is the sure guarantee of the resurrection of the members).

themes: for if we believe that Jesus died and rose again
them also which sleep in Jesus will God bring with him
the risen Christ the pledge of his people's resurrection
the Head's resurrection guaranteeing the members'

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the prophets, wishing to show the credibility of their words, said before all else 'the vision which Isaiah saw,' 'the word of the Lord which came to Jeremiah,' 'thus saith the Lord'; so Paul, speaking of a matter beyond human knowledge, grounds it 'by the word of the Lord,' a divine revelation; Paul declaring, by the word of the Lord, that those alive at Christ's coming shall not prevent (precede) those who are asleep — the divine assurance that the living shall not outstrip the dead (Paul, to settle a question only God could answer, speaking 'by the word of the Lord,' by direct revelation not his own opinion, and assuring the bereaved that the believers found living at Christ's coming will have no advantage over the dead, will not go before them into glory, for the sleeping saints shall be raised and share fully in the great reunion, losing nothing by having died first).

themes: for this we say unto you by the word of the Lord
we which are alive and remain unto the coming of the Lord
shall not prevent them which are asleep
the dead losing no advantage at Christ's coming

schools.summary: Gill: the Lord shall descend not by proxy or by representatives, not by the ministry of angels (as on Mount Sinai), nor by the ministers of the word (as under the gospel dispensation), nor by his Spirit (in which sense he descends spiritually to visit his people), but in person, in his human nature, in soul and body, visibly and gloriously, from heaven where he now is; Paul declaring that the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, the voice of the archangel, and the trump of God, and the dead in Christ shall rise first — the majestic coming of Christ and the resurrection of his sleeping people (Paul painting the scene of the second advent: the Lord 'himself,' not by deputy, descending in person from heaven, attended by a 'shout,' a cry of command, 'the voice of the archangel,' and 'the trump of God,' the great signals summoning the dead, at which 'the dead in Christ,' the believing departed, 'shall rise first,' raised before the living are changed, so the sleeping saints are first cared for).

themes: the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout
with the voice of the archangel, and with the trump of God
the dead in Christ shall rise first
the glorious descent and first resurrection

schools.summary: Barnes: the word 'then' (epeita) does not necessarily mean immediately but marks succession in time — afterwards, next in the order of events; after the dead are raised, the living shall be caught up; Paul declaring that then those alive and remaining shall be caught up together with the risen dead in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air, and so be ever with him — the great reunion and the everlasting presence with the Lord (Paul completing the scene: after the dead in Christ are raised, the believers then living 'caught up,' suddenly by divine power snatched away, 'together with them,' the raised dead and the living united in one company, 'in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air,' welcoming their descending King, and crowning all with the sweetest comfort, 'so shall we ever be with the Lord,' the saints, dead and living, gathered forever into the unbroken, eternal presence of Christ).

themes: then we which are alive and remain shall be caught up together with them
in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air
and so shall we ever be with the Lord
the great reunion and everlasting presence

schools.summary: Gill: they were to comfort one another with these words, or doctrines — that the saints, when they die, do not cease to be but are asleep, and asleep in Jesus; that their souls are with him and their bodies sleep in his arms and are his care; that these will be as soon with Christ as the saints alive at his coming; that the coming of Christ will be with great power and glory; Paul charging them to comfort one another with these words — the doctrine of the coming given for the comfort of the bereaved (Paul showing the great end of all he has revealed about the coming of the Lord and the resurrection: not for idle speculation about times and manner but 'wherefore comfort one another with these words,' that believers, and especially the bereaved, might console and strengthen each other with these truths, so the doctrine of Christ's return is, above all, a fountain of comfort for those who mourn their sleeping saints).

themes: wherefore comfort one another
with these words
the doctrine of the coming given for comfort
a fountain of comfort for the bereaved
